artifacts.content:
Hi Michelle,

I just made tea and realized I haven’t actually written you a proper update in ages, beyond our random memes.

I’ve been circling this whole “volunteering with seniors/rehab” thing again. It still goes back to Bà Ngoại and those years at St. Vincent’s Langara and Brock Fahrni. I was rereading my old 2021 visit notes from Brock Fahrni last week – all those scribbles about her late‑stage Alzheimer’s, which staff were on, what music calmed her down – and it brought back the guilt spiral from when she died at Langara in March 2021.

I ended up pulling out my therapy notebook from when I was seeing Dr. Melissa Chow at Pacific Coast Wellness Clinic that May–October 2021. There’s this line I’d underlined: “You can’t go back to visit more, but you can decide how present you want to be for other elders now.” That’s basically what pushed me to start looking at Carewest after I moved to Calgary last year.

It also doesn’t help that Dad’s stuff at Burnaby Hospital in Oct–Nov 2022 is still so vivid. Those three weeks on the rehab unit after his minor stroke, all the calls with Dr. Kaur about his blood thinners… I remember how different the unit felt when volunteers were around vs. when it was just you, me, Mom and exhausted nurses.

And then there’s my own brain. When Dr. Anita Patel at Marda Loop screened me for moderate depression in June 2023 and told me I needed more in‑person connection outside PrairieLynx, I sort of nodded and then… mostly kept working. So I’m trying to actually act on that now.

Anyway, this is long. How’s marking season? Still drowning in essays?

Love,
Andi

metadata.subject: Why I keep coming back to seniors volunteering

artifacts.content:
Journal – 2024-10-12

Couldn’t sleep, so trying to dump everything swirling around in my head.

I keep thinking about how many threads are tangled up in this urge to be in long‑term care/rehab spaces. There’s Grandma Hoa’s last year (April 2019–March 2020) in that Vancouver care home – the missed baths, the time she fell going to the washroom alone. I can still picture sitting with Dì Thuy on May 12, 2019, drafting that complaint letter to the facility manager about short staffing. Part of me is still angry; part of me just wants to be that “extra layer” of presence for someone else’s grandma so they’re not trying to shuffle to the bathroom alone.

Then Daniel’s uncle in the palliative unit at Richmond Hospital Jan–Mar 2020. All those late‑night drives, watching the nurses juggle pain med adjustments while volunteers quietly sat with him so his sister could go home for a shower. That’s the exact kind of steady, non‑medical support I feel drawn to.

Layer on top of that: Dad’s minor stroke and three weeks in Burnaby Hospital rehab in October 2022; Mom’s Zoom physio from Burnaby General outpatient most weekdays at 1 p.m. Pacific; Dì Thuy’s own minor stroke at Richmond Hospital in Oct 2022 and the 30–40 minute Fraser River dyke walks every other Sunday since late 2022; Daniel’s depressive episode and SSRI change at South Calgary PCN in January 2023; my own moderate depression screening with Dr. Patel in June 2023.

No wonder “relationship‑based volunteering” feels like the only thing that makes emotional sense right now.

Remember: balance this with back limits from the May 2023 Macleod Trail accident – no more than 2 hours on my feet (Lifemark’s rule).

metadata.title: Why I’m so fixated on continuing care spaces
metadata.location: 7423 Elbow Drive SW, Apartment 304, Calgary, AB

artifacts.content:
Event: Week planning – work, Carewest, family health
Time: Monday, 2025-02-10, 7:30–8:15 a.m.
Location: Kitchen table, Elbow Dr SW

Agenda / Notes:

1. Work (PrairieLynx Software Inc.)
- Log on early (6:30 a.m.) Mon–Fri so I can take longer mid‑day breaks.
- Block Tues 1:45–3:00 p.m. MT for Mom’s Zoom physio from Burnaby General Hospital outpatient (1:00 p.m. Pacific). Join via her iPad; help translate tech instructions.

2. Volunteering
- Ideal Carewest shifts: 1.5–3 hours, once or twice a week.
- Prefer Mon/Fri mornings (9:30–12:00) or Tues early afternoon (12:30–2:30), but MUST wrap by 2:00 p.m. on Thursdays because of Daniel’s CBT.
- Physical limit: per Lifemark Calgary Southwest physio (post‑Macleod Trail accident May 2023), avoid standing/walking >2 hours at a stretch.

3. Daniel
- Weekly CBT for work‑related burnout at Richmond Road Diagnostic & Treatment Centre: Thursdays 3:00–5:00 p.m. through at least June 2025. Leave apartment by 2:20.
- Keep memoir‑writing circle essay (“Walking Out of Winter”) updated – includes Glenmore Reservoir walks + his SSRI dosage change at South Calgary Primary Care Network in Jan 2023.

4. Family
- Monthly WhatsApp call: set up Dad’s Burnaby Hospital cardiology follow‑up (post‑stent) – 30–45 mins, coordinate with Michelle.
- Trips to Vancouver every six weeks (Aug 2024–June 2025) to help Dì Thuy clear Bà Ngoại’s belongings from St. Vincent’s Langara – block 4 days; adjust Carewest shifts accordingly.

5. Personal
- Tues 7:00–8:30 p.m.: adult children of hoarders support group (Dr. Kaur, Calgary Family Wellness Centre, online) – plan low‑key Wednesday mornings.

Hi Mom,

I booked Dad’s next Burnaby Hospital cardiologist video follow‑up for April 18 at 6 p.m. your time. I’ll call you and Ba on WhatsApp first from here, then loop in the cardiac clinic and Michelle like we’ve been doing since spring 2022. Please keep the list of his post‑stent meds handy so we can ask about the dizziness.

How are your own physio sessions going? I’ve kept my work schedule at PrairieLynx shifted earlier so I can join most weekdays at 1:00 p.m. Pacific for the Burnaby General Hospital outpatient Zoom. It actually works well with what I’m trying to do here – I can volunteer late mornings for 1.5–2 hours and still be back online in time to log into your physio.

I’ve also been looking more seriously at Carewest. After Dad’s minor stroke and those three weeks in the rehab unit at Burnaby Hospital in October–November 2022, I really saw how much calmer the days felt when volunteers were on the unit. And I keep thinking about Bà Ngoại’s last year – the missed baths and that fall at her Vancouver care home that led to the complaint Dì Thuy and I filed on May 12, 2019. I can’t fix the past, but maybe I can be an extra pair of eyes and ears for someone else’s mom.

Don’t worry, I’m being careful with my back since the Macleod Trail accident last May. The Lifemark Calgary Southwest physio said no more than 2 hours on my feet, so I’ll only take shorter shifts.

We’re doing comfort baking again this Saturday after [friend]’s Foothills mental health appointment – probably Vietnamese coffee flan. I’ll send pictures.

Love you,
Andi

metadata.subject: Dad’s heart call + my schedule here

artifacts.content:
Andi: Hey, still on for comfort baking this Saturday after your Foothills appointment?

Friend: Yeah, my outpatient group at Foothills Medical Centre mental health program wraps around 1:30, so I can be at your place on Elbow by 2.

Andi: Perfect. Let’s do the Vietnamese coffee flan again – it’s become our second‑Saturday‑of‑the‑month tradition since Feb 2023.

Friend: My therapist will be happy I’m not just going home to doom‑scroll lol.

Andi: Same. Dr. Patel at Marda Loop would probably high‑five me for actually having in‑person plans. When she screened me for moderate depression back in June 2023, she literally said, “You need people who aren’t on screens.”

Friend: She’s not wrong.

Andi: I’m trying to take it seriously. Thinking about committing to weekly volunteering at Carewest – maybe a rehab or leisure program. Ever since Dad’s stroke and those three weeks in Burnaby Hospital rehab in Oct–Nov 2022, I’ve had this picture of being the calm person who shows up to play cards or walk the hallway.

Friend: That sounds very you.

Andi: Logistically, I can probably do 1.5–2 hr shifts. I start PrairieLynx at 6:30 a.m. so I can take longer mid‑day breaks for Mom’s Burnaby General Zoom physio at 1 p.m. Pacific. Plus Lifemark said no more than 2 hrs standing since the Macleod Trail accident.

Friend: Look at you, balancing everyone’s rehab schedules including your own.

Andi: Lol, it’s a full‑time job on top of my full‑time job.

Friend: Okay, I’ll bring condensed milk. See you Sat.

Hi Daniel,

You were half‑asleep when I left the room, so I’ll write this down before I forget.

I sketched out a rough weekly rhythm that might actually be sustainable for both of us. Mondays and Fridays: I start PrairieLynx at 6:30, do a focused block, then aim for a 2–3 hour Carewest shift 9:30–12ish. That leaves time to decompress before Mom’s Burnaby General physio Zoom (2 p.m. our time most weekdays) and whatever random project emergencies pop up.

Tuesdays I’ll keep protecting early afternoon for deep work or, if it works out, a regular Carewest visit – maybe one‑to‑one reading or music. It would dovetail nicely with the memoir‑writing circle at the library in the evenings. I read part of the “Walking Out of Winter” draft last week – the section about your depressive episode, our late‑night Glenmore Reservoir walks, and how your doc at South Calgary Primary Care Network finally adjusted your SSRI in January 2023 – and people actually teared up.

Thursdays stay sacred for you: no volunteering after 2 p.m. so we can get to Richmond Road Diagnostic & Treatment Centre for your CBT (3–5 p.m.) through June 2025.

I’m also building in Vancouver trips every six weeks to help Dì Thuy sort Bà Ngoại’s things at St. Vincent’s Langara. We started in August 2024 and it’ll probably run to at least June 2025. On those weeks I’ll just pause any Carewest shifts.

Writing it all out makes it feel less chaotic. We’re juggling a lot – your burnout, my back from the Macleod Trail accident, both our families’ health stuff – but it also feels like we’re slowly building something intentional here.

Sleep now. We can talk details over coffee.

Love,
A

metadata.subject: Rough weekly rhythm so we don’t burn out

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – caregiving + connection

Things I’m already doing that line up with “leisure, one‑to‑one visiting, rehab/recovery, community programs”:

- Weekly reminiscence + music Zoom with Mom (Lan) and Dì Thuy on Sundays. We talk about visiting Grandma Hoa at St. Vincent’s: Brock Fahrni during her final year with late‑stage Alzheimer’s in 2021, share stories, and sometimes I pull out the private scrapbook I kept of those visits – dates, staff names, her behaviours, which songs helped.

- Every other Sunday (when I’m in BC), 30–40 minute gentle walks with Dì Thuy along the Fraser River dyke trail in Richmond, helping rebuild her stamina after her minor stroke at Richmond Hospital in October 2022.

- Monthly WhatsApp cardiology calls I set up from my Calgary apartment between Ba in Burnaby, Michelle in Coquitlam, and the Burnaby Hospital cardiologist to review his post‑stent meds.

- Memoir‑writing circle at Calgary Public Library since May 2023 – workshopping the essay about supporting Daniel through his depressive episode, Glenmore Reservoir walks, and the January 2023 SSRI adjustment at South Calgary Primary Care Network.

- Comfort baking afternoons at 7423 Elbow Dr SW since Feb 2023 with Daniel and [friend] after her Foothills Medical Centre mental health appointments – Vietnamese coffee flan, cookies, etc.

- My own supports: Dr. Anita Patel’s moderate depression screening (Marda Loop, June 2023) and the adult children of hoarders group with Dr. Kaur at Calgary Family Wellness Centre (Tues 7–8:30 p.m., Jan–Mar 2025 blocks).

All of this reassures me I can handle 1.5–3 hour Carewest shifts weekly, as long as I respect the <2‑hour standing limit from Lifemark after the May 2023 Macleod Trail accident.
